response: # Verdict: ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS ## Overall Assessment This component demonstrates a **solid foundation** in accessibility design with proper skip link implementation, semantic landmarks (nav/main/footer), correct list semantics for navigation, and visible focus indicators. However, the absence of `aria-current` on active navigation items represents a meaningful gap that affects screen reader users' ability to understand their current location without reading all nav links sequentially. ## Pre-commitment Predictions | Prediction | Actual Finding | |------------|-----------------| | Skip link present and functional ✓ | Confirmed - properly implemented with CSS :focus reveal pattern | | Semantic landmarks (nav, main, footer) ✓ | All three semantic elements correctly used | | Navigation aria-labels present ✓ | `aria-label="Main navigation"` on nav element | | Focus indicators visible ✓ | 3px solid outline meets WCAG 2.4.7 requirements | | Missing aria-current enhancement ⚠️ | **Confirmed as gap** - active page not marked in nav | ## Critical Findings No critical accessibility barriers identified. Component blocks no user category from accessing core functionality. ``` ### A11y Evidence Finding finding_id: skip-link-focus-reveal-001 fingerprint: a7b3c9d2e8f4a6b5c1d0e9f8g7h6i5j4k3l2m1n0o9p8q7r6s5t4u3v2w1x0y source: src/MainLayout.tsx (skip-link CSS) wcag_or_apg: WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order, WAI-ARIA Best Practice for Skip Links section_508_fpc_context: Not in scope - skip link pattern is standard best practice severity: ENHANCEMENT perspective_alarms: screen-reader=LOW, keyboard-only=LOW, low-vision=MEDIUM, cognitive=LOW evidence: CSS .skip-link:focus { top: 0; } reveals on focus via position:absolute/top:-40px pattern reproduction_steps: Tab to skip link → observe visual reveal and page jump expected_behavior: Skip link hidden off-screen until focused, then visible at top of viewport actual_behavior: Matches expected behavior - properly implemented CSS :focus reveal trend: stable ``` ## Major Findings 1. **Missing aria-current on active navigation item** — `src/MainLayout.tsx` (nav links) lacks `aria-current="page"` attribute to indicate current page location in the menu structure. This affects screen reader users who need context about where they are without reading all nav items sequentially. Per WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value and WAI-ARIA Navigation pattern, active navigation item should be marked with `aria-current="page"`. - Confidence: HIGH - Why this matters: Screen reader users lose orientation when navigating between pages; they cannot determine current location without reading all nav links or relying on page title alone. This increases cognitive load and reduces efficiency for keyboard/screen reader workflows. - Fix: Add `aria-current="page"` to the active navigation link (e.g., `Home`). When using React Router, this can be conditionally rendered based on current route match. ## Minor Findings - **No skip-to-top/back-link in footer** — Footer lacks a "Back to top" or "Return to navigation" link that could help keyboard users quickly return from long-form content sections without reverse-tabbing through all main content (WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks). This is optional enhancement rather than requirement for this component type. ## Enhancements - Consider adding `aria-current="page"` dynamically based on current route - Add skip-to-top link in footer or header for long-form pages with multiple sections - If navigation items have icons, ensure decorative icons use `aria-hidden="true"` to
